source.title: Pain-Related Expectation and Prediction Error Signals in the Anterior Insula Are Not Related to Aversiveness

confidence_rationale: Directly tests whether the pain PE signal in ventral anterior insula is pain-specific or a general aversive-event signal by crossing painful stimuli with aversive pictures in a cross-modal expectation paradigm. Pain-specific PE, not general aversiveness, drives the ventral anterior insula integration. Strong design, clean fMRI dissociation. Most relevant for arguing that the pain forward model must be modality-specific at the integration stage.
failure_signatures: A fully shared aversive-event forward model is falsified by modality-specific PE signals.
If a single forward model generated both pain PE and non-pain aversive PE, ventral anterior insula should respond to both; it does not.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The ventral anterior insula integrates pain intensity with pain-specific expectations and prediction errors. Modality-mismatched cues (aversive picture cue paired with pain delivery) eliminate the integration signal. The effect is pain-selective, not general-aversive.
mapping.ree_translation: The forward model that generates the PE consumed by SD-032b must be modality-specific to pain. A generic aversiveness predictor is insufficient. Within the pain family, this argues against collapsing across sub-modalities (affective vs sensory-discriminative) but does not resolve whether those two share a common trunk.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Fazeli distinguishes pain from non-pain aversive events, not within-pain sub-modalities. It establishes modality-specificity at the coarse level. It is not a direct test of E2_harm_a vs E2_harm_s separation.
mapping.source_context: Human fMRI, cross-modal expectancy paradigm (pain vs aversive pictures), healthy males.

confidence_components.notes: Mapping fidelity lowered because it speaks to between-modality specificity (pain vs pictures), not within-modality sub-stream separation (A-delta vs C). Still strong because it shows the predictive machinery is at least stream-indexed.
